quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: BPT is the entry-level degree pathway described in the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare/NCAHP Physiotherapy Handbook for preparing physiotherapy graduates to assess, manage and help optimise movement, function and quality of life.

quick_facts.label: Standard duration
quick_facts.value: The handbook specifies four academic years (or eight semesters) followed by a compulsory one-year full-time rotatory clinical internship.

quick_facts.label: Typical entry baseline
quick_facts.value: The handbook specifies 10+2/equivalent with Physics, Chemistry and Biology and 50% aggregate, plus age 17 in the current year; institutions and applicable state rules can impose additional conditions.

quick_facts.label: Admission route
quick_facts.value: BPT admission is not uniformly NEET-only nationally: the handbook provides for an entrance test conducted by a university, government or other competent authority and states that admission can also occur through NEET. Applicants must follow the current prospectus and counselling rules of the specific institution or state.

quick_facts.label: Regulatory context
quick_facts.value: Physiotherapy Professional is a recognised category in the National Commission for Allied and Healthcare Professions Act, 2021; the Act provides for standards, institutional recognition and Central/State registers.

quick_facts.label: Direct postgraduate progression
quick_facts.value: The handbook sets out MPT as a two-year/four-semester programme and lists specialities including musculoskeletal, neuroscience, cardio-pulmonary, sports, paediatrics and neonatal, community, and oncological physiotherapy.

sections.heading: Eligibility for BPT
paragraphs: Under the national Physiotherapy Handbook’s stated selection procedure, an applicant should have passed Higher Secondary (10+2) or an equivalent recognised qualification with Physics, Chemistry and Biology and at least 50% aggregate marks. It also specifies that the applicant must be 17 years old in the current year and submit a physical-fitness certificate. For overseas or open-school qualifications, the handbook gives additional subject/equivalence conditions. Because admission is administered by universities, states and competent authorities, candidates should treat the current institutional prospectus as controlling for reservations, category relaxations, domicile, subject combinations and document requirements.
bullets: Academic baseline: 10+2/equivalent with PCB and 50% aggregate under the handbook’s selection procedure.
Age baseline: 17 years in the current year under the handbook.
Check the relevant university/state notification for any current variations or additional requirements.

sections.heading: Duration and clinical internship
paragraphs: The handbook’s minimum framework describes BPT as four years of academic training—either an annual pattern or eight semesters—plus a one-year, full-time rotatory clinical internship. It separately specifies the internship as 12 months and at least 2,016 hours, with rotations through outpatient and inpatient clinical facilities. The handbook also places the entry-level physiotherapy degree at a minimum five-year pathway including internship.
bullets: Internship: compulsory 1-year full-time rotatory clinical internship after passing the required examinations.
Internship minimum stated in the handbook: 2,016 hours.

sections.heading: What the curriculum covers
paragraphs: The national handbook is competency-based rather than limited to classroom theory. Its stated BPT outcomes include examination, assessment, evaluation and physiotherapeutic management of disease, disorders and trauma; health promotion; ethical practice; communication; evidence-informed decision-making; teamwork; and service management. The published scheme includes foundational sciences such as anatomy, physiology and biochemistry, alongside exercise modalities, electro-physical agents, psychology and sociology, healthcare delivery, clinical orientation, pathology, pharmacology, public health and health promotion. Exact subject sequencing, examinations and credit structures can vary where a university implements its own approved scheme.
bullets: Foundations: anatomy, physiology, biochemistry, psychology and sociology.
Professional and clinical learning: exercise modalities, electro-physical agents, clinical orientation, pathology, pharmacology and emergency-care skills.
Population-health component: public health and health promotion.
Training model: competency-based learning with practical/clinical skill development and internship.

sections.heading: How admission works, including NEET-UG
paragraphs: The handbook states that BPT admission should be based on eligibility and an entrance test, with the test conducted by the university, government or another competent authority; it also states that a candidate can be admitted through NEET. Therefore, NEET-UG is an important possible route, but it should not be represented as a universal national requirement for every BPT seat. Selection, counselling, merit rules, deadlines, seat categories and whether NEET is accepted or required are determined by the current notification of the relevant university, state authority or college. For context, the National Testing Agency’s 2026 notice archive records NEET-UG 2026 activity, but candidates must rely on the applicable admission authority rather than assume one examination route fits all BPT programmes.
bullets: Read the current BPT prospectus before applying; do not rely solely on general course listings.
Where an institution uses NEET, follow its stated score, counselling and document rules.
Where an institution conducts its own or a state/competent-authority test, follow that route and timetable instead.

sections.heading: Post-BPT study and career directions
paragraphs: A direct specialist progression is Master of Physiotherapy (MPT). The handbook describes MPT admission as requiring a recognised BPT/BSc Physiotherapy qualification with 50% pass marks and specifies a two-year/four-semester duration; it lists specialities such as musculoskeletal, neuroscience, cardio-pulmonary, sports, paediatrics and neonatal, obstetrics and gynaecology, community, and oncological physiotherapy. Graduates may also pursue physiotherapist roles in settings identified by the handbook, including hospitals, outpatient clinics, rehabilitation centres, community-health settings, schools, occupational-health centres, sports centres/clubs and physiotherapist private offices or clinics. Before choosing employment or entrepreneurship, verify the institution’s recognised status and the applicable Central/State registration requirements, because the NCAHP Act provides for standards, recognition and professional registers.
bullets: Specialise through MPT, subject to the university’s current admission criteria and entrance process.
Work across clinical rehabilitation, hospitals, outpatient care, community health, occupational health, education/research and sports settings.
Private clinic/practice is a possible work setting identified in the handbook; compliance obligations and local registration requirements should be checked before practice.
